verdict_reasoning: HSPD1 (Hsp60/GroEL) is a mitochondrial matrix chaperonin with a canonical intracellular location — essential for protein folding in the mitochondrial matrix. However, documented evidence establishes a non-baseline surface pool: (1) tumor cells display HSPD1 on the outer leaflet as an ecto-chaperone, recognized by cancer-directed antibodies and immune programs; (2) apoptotic/necroptotic cells externalize HSPD1 during programmed cell death; (3) some immune-activated dendritic cells show HSPD1 surface exposure linked to innate immune signaling. The NCBI summary explicitly names a signaling role in innate immunity, consistent with stress-state and cell-death-induced ecto-display. The canonical subcellular home is mitochondrial-matrix, but the documented non-baseline surface pool in cancer and cell-death contexts qualifies as contextual surface accessibility.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The relative abundance and functional significance of the cancer-cell and cell-death-induced surface pools are not fully quantified in the resolver context; confidence rests on convergent evidence across cancer, apoptosis, and immune-activation literature rather than direct surface-biotinylation or proteomics data on primary cells.
